occurrences.context: **Watch-item (release rules vs Warden):** `releaseRuleSchema` ({id, enabled, severity, description, facts, intent}) is structurally a governance rule, mirroring Warden without being Warden. ADR draft (release-provenance-as-lifecycle-projection) consciously framed release as a lifecycle *projection* (diff-provenance) distinct from Warden's state-validation axis, and exposed it as a `release.check` trail rather than a new primitive — which is correct and accepted. But two parallel rule-eval vocabularies is the ceiling. **Trigger: if a third "rules" engine appears, unify them under one governance substrate.** Two is coincidence; three is fragmentation (fights "additions strengthen primitives, not fragment them").

occurrences.context: - **Surfaces are adapters, not the core: half right, wrong word.** Surfaces aren't core; they are peers. But *adapter* is the wrong noun. An adapter translates between two systems with independent shapes; it can drift from what it adapts. A projection is a rendering of the same contract; it structurally cannot drift. Adopting "adapter" weakens the very guarantee that makes surfaces-as-peers hold.

occurrences.context: **The scoring frame:** An agent working *inside this repo* is at roughly 8.5/10 — queryable graph facts (Wayfinder), CI-verified docs (`docs:snippets`, `warden:agents:check`, `error-taxonomy:check`), enforced single-meaning vocabulary, a self-declared documentation authority hierarchy (tenets → ADRs → fieldguides → contributing → AGENTS.md), and in-repo agent workflow guidance (Subagent Rules, Brief Discipline). An agent in a *consumer* project is closer to 6/10. The gap is almost entirely that the repo's best legibility machinery has not been projected outward through `trails create` — a one-write-many-reads problem where the write exists and the consumer-side reads don't.

occurrences.context: **Trails owns facts and derivation; Skillset owns lowering and distribution.** The warden guide is generated by Trails from the live rule manifest (`trails warden guide --manifest`) — that derivation must stay Trails-owned, because the facts are Trails contracts. Skillset consumes the generated output as a shared partial and projects it into every surface (SKILL.md, AGENTS.md, Codex equivalents). This is one-write-many-reads with a clean division: if Skillset ever needs to *understand* Trails facts to do its job, the seam is in the wrong place.
occurrences.reason: Preserve authored migration plans and historical decision/release evidence while keeping occurrences visible to the run ledger.

occurrences.context: Corollary for skill content: derive the reference layer (command catalogs, rule indexes, error taxonomies — things that exist as queryable Trails facts), author the pedagogy (narrative, examples-as-teaching, pitfalls). Over-generation is the failure mode to guard against: a skill that is 100% projection reads like a man page, not a teacher.
occurrences.reason: Preserve authored migration plans and historical decision/release evidence while keeping occurrences visible to the run ledger.

occurrences.context: 1. **Skill content becomes part-projection from live framework facts.** This is the big one. CLI mode consumes static source files; library mode lets Trails *synthesize Skillset source in memory* from contracts: operator topo → skill command-coverage sections (the run/add/lifecycle gap becomes derived instead of hand-authored and forever-stale); warden manifest → rule reference; error taxonomy → error-handling reference; wayfind catalog → query guidance. The skill stops being prose that drifts from the CLI and becomes a projection of it — the same move Trails made for MCP tools and CLI flags, applied to agent teaching material.
occurrences.reason: Preserve authored migration plans and historical decision/release evidence while keeping occurrences visible to the run ledger.

occurrences.context: 5. **In-process projection tests.** Trails' own `bun test` can assert "the skill compiled from current manifests teaches command X" without process spawning — fast, deterministic, and it makes skill coverage a *testable contract* (which is the precondition for putting skill-content claims in CI at all).

occurrences.context: CLI-mode Skillset compiles static source files a human authored. Library-mode Skillset lets a host program synthesize that source from live facts and consume the outputs as data. For Trails that converts skill content from drift-prone prose into part-projection (operator topo → command coverage, warden manifest → rule reference), enables scaffold-time tailored guidance inside `trails create`, and makes lock-hash provenance the oracle for safe guidance refresh. Details and the Trails-owns-facts/Skillset-owns-lowering seam live in the adoption note; this note is about what Skillset must *be* for any of that to work.
occurrences.reason: Preserve authored migration plans and historical decision/release evidence while keeping occurrences visible to the run ledger.

occurrences.context: 5. **Typed diagnostics, not thrown strings.** Lint/check/build problems as structured values with severity, source location, and a stable code — so a host can wrap them as its own check (Trails would project them into a release-smoke check or Warden-adjacent gate) instead of parsing stderr. Stable diagnostic codes matter more than message text: hosts gate on codes.

occurrences.context: This session's Warden run emitted `dead-internal-trail` for all 17 `wayfind.*` trails (`packages/wayfinder/src/queries.ts:1112-1526`). Verified: all 17 declare `visibility: 'internal'`; the rule's only escape hatches are `on:` activation or compose evidence (`packages/warden/src/rules/dead-internal-trail.ts:95-97,137-142`); nothing inspects MCP facet projection even though the trails are reachable exactly that way (`apps/trails/src/mcp-options.ts:38-46`); and no suppression, acceptance note, or TRL-915 reference exists anywhere in the repo. The project's own done-criteria say "known Warden/Wayfinder warnings are either fixed or explicitly accepted as non-blocking" — this is the bulk of them. The three remaining warnings (`signal-graph-coaching` on `demo.entity-store`) also need an accept-or-fix call; they live in the private `trails-demo` app. My recommendation: teach `dead-internal-trail` that **surface-facet include-ID projection counts as reachability** (it is declared, reviewable contract — coherent with the `surface-facet-coherence` rule already governing those maps), rather than a blanket suppression or reshaping the catalog.

occurrences.context: Residual input is the core primitive underneath the user-facing projection, but the lower-level machinery is **input assembly**: flags, schema defaults, env/config, prior trail outputs, example-derived suggestions, answers files, and interview answers are all just **sources contributing toward one validated input object**. The interview is whatever is left after assembly. This one sentence unifies flags+interview, defaults+interview, output→input auto-fill, agent missing-input errors, HTML form prefill, permit confirmation, and provenance.

occurrences.context: - **Layer inputs are part of the residual, but only when user-satisfiable.** Typed layers can declare input schemas, and surfaces already project them (the `layer-field-name-drift` rule exists because of it). Input assembly must account for layer-declared fields too, or interviews will ask for trail fields while silently missing layer fields. But not every layer field should be promptable: auth/session/tracing/runtime fields may be internal machinery. Interview asks only for layer fields that are public, user-satisfiable, and surface-projectable. One assembly, one residual, trail + eligible layers together.

occurrences.context: **Boundary invariant:** if Regrade ever rewrites a *projected* output, that's a smell — the projection should have re-derived. Reach stops at the **authored** layer (trail definitions, blaze bodies); projections follow by derivation. Mature Regrade migrates *less* than a generic codemod, not more. Two tiers, both pass the test: graph tier (re-author the contract, derivation re-fans-out) + authored-source tier (AST rewrite of the words a human wrote).

occurrences.context: `AGENTS.md`'s "Wayfinder First" framing presents Wayfinder as the saved-graph-facts surface — read the artifact, project facts. `outline` is the one wayfind query that parses **live source on every call**. That is defensible: the value *is* the source↔graph bridge, and Wayfinder is the graph's home, so the bridge belongs here. But the mental model ("Wayfinder = saved artifacts") now has an exception, and it should be named in the docs rather than silently true. One sentence in the Wayfinder doc / AGENTS "Wayfinder First" block: outline reads source directly and cross-references the saved graph.

occurrences.context: The decisive call: **the dot means "committed control," not "disposable."** Reached after weighing two coherent ways to make the dot mean exactly one thing (disposable vs committed-control); the original synthesis model failed only because it made the dot mean *both* (committed rules beside ignored cache). We picked committed-control because it gives one contained home for "what does this project do with Trails," uniform across src and flat layouts, and because the disposable meaning is recovered for free by moving disposable state out of the repo entirely (see below).
occurrences.reason: Preserve project as an ordinary count noun selected by a determiner or domain adjective.

occurrences.context: Hoist uniformly: any scope that owns a Trails project keeps `trails.config.*` + `trails.lock` at its own root (matching the per-package `package.json`/`tsconfig.json` norm), while `.trails/` holds only what has no root convention — rules and grown sections — at every scope. This dissolves any root-vs-nested asymmetry and yields a free signal: a scope with a hoisted `trails.config.*` *is* its own project root; a scope with only `.trails/rules/` is a centralized-workspace member contributing scope-local rules (it inherits the root's config). Hoisting never competes with authored `trails/` code, because `trails.*` (control) and bare `trails/` (primitive) are distinct by morphology.
occurrences.reason: Preserve project as an attributive noun before established repository and workspace concepts.

occurrences.context: Surfaces deliberately do *not* get a `src/` content dir. A surface opening is the boundary between the pure graph and the outside world — mostly projection wiring — so openings live in `bin/` (the opening and the executable entry are the same honest unit). The authored part of a surface (exposure + accommodations: aliases, input mappings, facets, per ADR-0050) attaches to the contract or `trails.config.*`, and *what's* exposed is already derivable from the topo. So there is nothing left to justify a `src/surfaces/` directory.

The `kind`-carrying schema (trail-id / symbol / vocabulary), plus three things the prose side needs that a `rg`-style rewrite cannot give: morphology-aware **discovery** (`from` surfaces neighbor forms you would miss, e.g. `blazes`), per-occurrence **judgment** (a packet of intent + context + preserve-cautions + labeled examples, not a regex), and a recursive **discover → triage → judge → verify** loop driven to a completion gate (zero capture-forms AND zero unclassified neighbors). Target mapping is **derive-by-default / declare-to-tighten / override-when-wrong**. Durable retirement (`retire: true`) writes to a **typed registry**, never markdown; the home is a project binding (Trails → lexicon-rendered), never an authored noun. The primary entry is the **command** (`trails regrade <from> <to>`, **dry-run by default**, `--apply` to execute, `--plan` to scaffold a file), with Warden as the safety net for raw renames. Built **on rails**: deterministic tooling (discovery/apply/verify) + a skill (orchestration, ledger) + an agent (judgment only).

occurrences.context: - **T2-e: topographer package — KEEP as-is (census verdict, 2026-07-07).** Three genuine cross-package consumers (warden 12+ files, wayfinder 7, apps/trails 18); coherent derivation+persistence kernel (derive and topo-store share the same projection helpers) ringed by durability utilities; the boundary IS accepted **ADR-0042** (Core/Topographer Boundary Doctrine), written precisely to keep persistence out of core — dissolving would reverse a settled ADR for zero user-facing gain (concept-toll issue already solved by Tier 3 demotion). No new deps or cycles would block a dissolve (bun:sqlite is type-only; DB engine already in core), so the barrier is doctrinal, not technical — and the doctrine is right. Mild smells noted, left alone: `workspace-topos` + `activation` clusters have 1 consumer each; `deriveTopoGraph` is in-memory/single-process (by ADR-0042's own literal test reads "core") but shared helpers bind it to the persistence kernel — not worth the churn. Migration cost of dissolve: large (~55 import sites + reopening the ADR). Keep: zero.
occurrences.reason: Preserve authored migration plans and historical decision/release evidence while keeping occurrences visible to the run ledger.

The ratified projection→derive+render split supplies the grammar's two anchor verbs; core's `project*` exports resolve into derive*/render* during the reset.

occurrences.context: The marker projection is deterministic, pathful, and loud for several unsupported schema constructs, but it is not yet a safe v1 stable marker contract. The current implementation accepts common Zod validation constraints and refinements while projecting the same marker as the unconstrained schema. That means two validation contracts can be semantically different while sharing a version marker.

occurrences.context: The metadata checker, installed-skill drift checker, and Claude `SessionStart` hook are scoped and non-mutating by default. Metadata sync has a clear two-source policy: plugin manifest version owns plugin semver, `packages/core/package.json` owns the framework target, and derived marketplace/skill copies are checked or synchronized. The installed-skill checker reports copy/symlink/missing state, file drift, stale vocabulary, and `metadata.trails.version` drift without changing global files. The hook stays silent outside likely Trails projects and suggests only Warden probes with `--lock cached --no-lock-mutation`.

occurrences.context: **What's the relationship between `trails init` and `outfitter init`?** `trails init` creates a Trails project (action registry, surface adapters, contract governance). `outfitter init` sets up dev tooling (tsconfig, lefthook, oxlint). A Trails project would run both. They should compose: `trails init` could optionally call `outfitter init` for dev tooling setup, or the developer runs them independently.

occurrences.context: Add the export-restructure Regrade class family (TRL-1210). `export-restructure:cli-aliases` inverts legacy `cliAliases`/`trailsCliAliases` exports into `surfaceOverlay({ cli })` bindings inside the module's `trailsOverlays` export — adding the `@ontrails/core` import, deleting the legacy export, and routing anything it cannot prove safe (computed keys, spreads, in-module `aliases:` references) to `needs-review` with the exact target shape named. `export-restructure:mcp-trailheads` projects call-site MCP trailhead maps into `surfaceOverlay({ mcp })` group bindings: it rewrites in place when the same module exports `trailsOverlays`, and otherwise emits a classified `needs-review` handoff naming the module-overlay target while the call-site map stays as the richer-metadata override-in-context. Warden's fix-class union grows to `'export-restructure' | 'term-rewrite'`, `no-legacy-cli-alias-export` now advertises the `export-restructure` class, and `loadWardenRegradeClasses` supersedes `loadWardenTermRewriteClasses` (still exported) as the full Warden-routed class loader. Class-mode Regrade also gains the full plan lifecycle: `trails regrade plan --type class --class-ids ...` writes a `.trails/regrade/<slug>.json` plan carrying class ids, scope, and intent, `regrade check` re-runs the dry run and gates on outstanding rewrites or review, and `regrade apply` applies and graduates the plan to `.trails/regrade/history/<slug>-<hash>.json` — the same plan → check → apply → history evidence trail vocabulary regrades already had, now available to structural transforms. The class family ships for downstream apps bridging the pre-1.0 gap, so pre-cutover alias exports and trailhead maps migrate mechanically instead of by hand.

occurrences.context: The OpenAPI projection now documents BlobRef routes as binary responses (`content: { '*/*': { schema: { type: 'string', format: 'binary' } } }`) instead of a JSON data envelope, matching the raw bytes the runtime serves with the blob's declared mimeType. Error responses keep the JSON error envelope. Both the fetch handler and the OpenAPI projection now share one BlobRef output recognition helper.

occurrences.context: Add `--token <token>` to `trails run` for permit resolution via the topo's auth adapter. New `tokenPreset()` exposes the flag; `'token'` is added to `META_FLAG_CANDIDATES`. The CLI resolves the `auth` resource through the shared permit-boundary helper, calls `adapter.authenticate({ surface: 'cli', bearerToken, requestId })`, and projects the resolved `Permit` into `ExecuteTrailOptions.permit` (added in TRL-408) so it reaches `ctx.permit`. Failures: missing adapter → `ValidationError` (exit 1); adapter returns `Err(AuthError)` or `Ok(null)` → `AuthError` (category `'auth'`, exit 9, with the adapter's code preserved on `error.context.code`). `--token` and `--permit` are mutually exclusive — supplying both yields `ValidationError`.

occurrences.context: Phase 7 starts: typed layers with optional object-shaped surface input. `Layer` gains an optional `input?: LayerInputSchema` field for surface projection (TRL-473/474 will project it onto CLI/MCP/HTTP). `executeTrail({ layers })` remains the canonical per-call wrapper option. Layers without `input` schemas stay surface-invisible and cover runtime-only concerns such as tenant guards, rate limiting, circuit breaking, and custom audit logging.

occurrences.context: Project typed-layer `input` schemas onto CLI flags. Each effective layer (topo + surface + trail composition order) with a non-undefined `input` schema gets its fields auto-derived into `--flag` options on every command it attaches to. Parsed values route to the layer at runtime via `ctx.extensions[LAYER_INPUTS_KEY][layer.name]` — `Layer.wrap` is unchanged. Collision rule: if a layer field name collides with a trail input field, another layer's projected name, or a CLI meta flag, the layer's flag is renamed to `--<layer-name>-<original-flag-name>` and a one-line warning emits to stderr (`[trails] ...`). Renames are deterministic across builds. New `LAYER_INPUTS_KEY` (exported from `@ontrails/core`) reserves the `ctx.extensions` slot.
occurrences.reason: Preserve authored migration plans and historical decision/release evidence while keeping occurrences visible to the run ledger.

occurrences.context: Project typed-layer `input` schemas onto MCP and HTTP surfaces. Closes Phase 7. Lifts `collectAttachedTypedLayers` and `projectLayerFieldName` (collision-rename rule) into `@ontrails/core/internal/layer-projection` so all three surfaces share one source of truth. The CLI surface refactors to consume the lifted helpers (no behavior change). MCP merges layer fields into each tool's `inputSchema` and partitions inbound args at invocation time. HTTP merges layer fields into the route's request schema (query for reads, body for writes) and exposes new optional `HttpRouteDefinition.inputSchema` + `layerInputProjections` for surface adapters / OpenAPI generators. Collision rule matches TRL-473's: deterministic rename to a layer-prefixed camelCase name with the original captured in the routing table. Side fix: MCP and HTTP handlers now forward `topoLayers: graph.layers` + `surfaceLayers: layers` so topo-scope layers actually compose at runtime (previously the handlers used the deprecated `layers` alias and never read `graph.layers`).
occurrences.reason: Preserve authored migration plans and historical decision/release evidence while keeping occurrences visible to the run ledger.

occurrences.context: - [`b077fb7`](https://github.com/outfitter-dev/trails/commit/b077fb7ba6d9724cac6f0e59bc3fec9aec28984c): Add the export-restructure Regrade class family (TRL-1210). `export-restructure:cli-aliases` inverts legacy `cliAliases`/`trailsCliAliases` exports into `surfaceOverlay({ cli })` bindings inside the module's `trailsOverlays` export — adding the `@ontrails/core` import, deleting the legacy export, and routing anything it cannot prove safe (computed keys, spreads, in-module `aliases:` references) to `needs-review` with the exact target shape named. `export-restructure:mcp-trailheads` projects call-site MCP trailhead maps into `surfaceOverlay({ mcp })` group bindings: it rewrites in place when the same module exports `trailsOverlays`, and otherwise emits a classified `needs-review` handoff naming the module-overlay target while the call-site map stays as the richer-metadata override-in-context. Warden's fix-class union grows to `'export-restructure' | 'term-rewrite'`, `no-legacy-cli-alias-export` now advertises the `export-restructure` class, and `loadWardenRegradeClasses` supersedes `loadWardenTermRewriteClasses` (still exported) as the full Warden-routed class loader. Class-mode Regrade also gains the full plan lifecycle: `trails regrade plan --type class --class-ids ...` writes a `.trails/regrade/<slug>.json` plan carrying class ids, scope, and intent, `regrade check` re-runs the dry run and gates on outstanding rewrites or review, and `regrade apply` applies and graduates the plan to `.trails/regrade/history/<slug>-<hash>.json` — the same plan → check → apply → history evidence trail vocabulary regrades already had, now available to structural transforms. The class family ships for downstream apps bridging the pre-1.0 gap, so pre-cutover alias exports and trailhead maps migrate mechanically instead of by hand.

occurrences.context: A trail is a typed contract — input schema, output schema, error types, examples, safety properties, composition graph — that has a blaze establishing how it runs. The contract is the product. The blaze makes it runnable. CLI, MCP, HTTP, and WebSocket surfaces render it for their contexts. You author the contract once. The framework projects it onto whatever surfaces you need.
occurrences.reason: Preserve projects as an ordinary plural repository or application noun selected by an established domain modifier.

occurrences.context: The Trails lexicon has been cut over several times in the pre-1.0 window. Cutovers were applied in place across the ADR record rather than threaded through supersession, because the project is still pre-release and the old terms would only create confusion for people reading through the ADR history or for agents consuming the docs. Each cutover has its own ADR documenting the decision; this section is a running log of where the in-place rewrites happened.
occurrences.reason: Preserve project as an ordinary count noun selected by a determiner or domain adjective.

occurrences.context: - **Cutover 3** ([ADR-0035](0035-surface-apis-render-the-graph.md)): Surface API cleanup. Retired `trailhead()` as the canonical one-liner in favor of `surface()`. Reframed `build*` helpers as `derive*` projections, standardized `create*` for runtime materialization, and made `graph` the canonical local name for topo instances in active docs and examples. Applied in place across the active ADR record using the same pre-1.0 precedent.
occurrences.reason: Preserve authored migration plans and historical decision/release evidence while keeping occurrences visible to the run ledger.

occurrences.context: Auth is policy, not projection. `derivePermit()` is dropped entirely — scopes are always explicitly authored. Convention-derived scopes sound convenient, but they create a mapping that's invisible to the developer and hard to audit. occurrences.context: The topo store projects the resolved topology into relational tables within `trails.db`: The foundational `topo_snapshots` table intentionally restates ADR-0014's core database primitive so this ADR can present the full topo store schema as a self-contained unit. occurrences.context: The framework already uses SQLite as its own internal database (see ADR: Core Database Primitive). The topo store projects the structural graph into `trails.db` for governance and introspection (see ADR: Topo Store). The patterns established there — Zod-to-SQLite type mapping, typed accessors, read-only resources, escape hatches for complex queries — are proven by the framework's own usage.

occurrences.context: Trails has a clear convention: core primitives are bare nouns (`trail()`, `store()`, `resource()`, `signal()`), and helpers that produce primitives use verb+noun form (`createTrailContext()`, `derivePermit()`). "Derive" is the framework's own verb for mechanical projection[^1] — trail IDs from schemas, CLI flags from fields, exit codes from errors. A function that mechanically projects a trail from a schema belongs in the same category.

occurrences.context: The naming wasn't always wrong. OpenAPI generation lived in `@ontrails/schema` until the precedent was set that "schema is for primitive schema declarations, not surface-specific projections," and OpenAPI was relocated to `@ontrails/http`.[^trl-586] What's left after that move isn't primitive schema declarations — it's durable artifacts derived from the graph. The package's content is one shape; its name describes another.
occurrences.reason: Preserve authored migration plans and historical decision/release evidence while keeping occurrences visible to the run ledger.

occurrences.context: But the topo store is the read-only *projection of a persisted snapshot* established by [ADR-0015: Topo Store](0015-topo-store.md). It writes rows to `.trails/state/trails.db`. It tracks history across builds. It produces TopoGraph and lock-manifest exports. None of that runs to dispatch a trail; the in-memory `ReadonlyMap` is the dispatch engine.[^adr-15-execution] What ships from core today as if it were a graph primitive is, in lifecycle terms, a tooling artifact.
occurrences.reason: Preserve authored migration plans and historical decision/release evidence while keeping occurrences visible to the run ledger.

occurrences.context: Both misfiles point at the same missing piece: there is no single, declared rule that says when something belongs in core and when it belongs in the package next door. occurrences.context: Project-local Warden rules live in `.trails/rules.ts` or direct `.trails/rules/*.ts` files at the project root. Warden loads those modules by default for command and API runs, so an adopting repo can enforce local migration or governance rules without shipping them from `@ontrails/warden`. The location is the ownership signal: a rule in that committed-control directory is project-local by placement, not by advisory metadata. Modules may export `rule`, `rules`, `sourceRule`, `sourceRules`, `topoRule`, or `topoRules`; rules without explicit metadata receive repo-local source-static or topo-aware execution metadata so short-lived migration rules do not need ceremony before they can run.

occurrences.context: Project-root discovery uses committed project markers such as `trails.config.*` or `trails.lock`, with source-shaped projects as fallback when no committed marker exists above them. A bare `.trails/` directory is not a root marker by itself. This lets Warden find root-local rules from nested cwd invocations without making every incidental `.trails/` folder look like a project boundary.

occurrences.context: Beta 16 split `@ontrails/commander` out of `@ontrails/cli/commander`, moved the topo-store API into `@ontrails/topographer`, added typed layers as a real primitive with attachment scopes, renamed surface-map artifacts to `TopoGraph` / `.trails/topo.lock`, added the `unmockable` resource marker, and absorbed pagination and date shortcuts into CLI surface derivation. Beta 17 added the example-driven surface-parity helper and the HTTP surface harness, and projected `inputSchema` v1 minimums for shipped surface entrypoints. Beta 18 added `@ontrails/http/fetch` and `@ontrails/http/bun` as a Web Fetch kernel plus a Bun-native HTTP binding. Beta 19 renamed the `cross` composition family to `compose`, promoted the topo artifact commands to top-level `trails compile` / `trails validate`, made trail versioning resolve at runtime across all surfaces, introduced the `@ontrails/adapter-kit` authoring toolchain, and added `warden --fix` for safe source fixes. Together those changes mean a downstream app upgrading from beta.15 should rethink CLI imports, MCP exposure, public output schemas, resource mocks, error taxonomy, observability, Topographer adoption, and trail-composition vocabulary, not just bump versions.
occurrences.reason: Preserve authored migration plans and historical decision/release evidence while keeping occurrences visible to the run ledger.

occurrences.context: Beta 19 renames the first-class trail composition family from `cross` to `compose` across core contracts, testing helpers, topo projections, Warden rules, and CLI scaffolds. `composes`, `ctx.compose`, `composeInput`, and `Compose*` type names are now the public authoring vocabulary. Topo persistence migrates legacy composition rows and graph keys forward on the next `trails compile`, so committed artifacts re-key themselves.
occurrences.reason: Preserve authored migration plans and historical decision/release evidence while keeping occurrences visible to the run ledger.

occurrences.context: Rename every `crosses:` declaration to `composes:`, every `ctx.cross(...)` call to `ctx.compose(...)`, and any imported `Cross*` / `crossInput` symbols to their `Compose*` / `composeInput` equivalents. After renaming, run `bun run compile` to re-key the committed `.trails/topo.lock`, then `bun run validate` to confirm no drift remains. (Scaffolded projects expose the framework commands as package scripts — `bun run compile`, `bun run validate`, `bun run diff` — so prefer those over `bun trails …`, which only works where a `trails` script exists; otherwise use `bunx trails …`.)
occurrences.reason: Preserve authored migration plans and historical decision/release evidence while keeping occurrences visible to the run ledger.

occurrences.context: Trail versioning is no longer authoring-only. Beta 19 resolves trail versions during execution — live revisions, forks, marker references, and unsupported-version errors — and projects live version metadata on CLI, HTTP, and MCP surfaces with explicit surface version selection threaded through shared execution. A version lifecycle CLI — `trails revise`, `trails deprecate` (with `--archive` to archive instead of deprecate), and `trails doctor` for diagnostics — and a top-level `trails diff` that surfaces version, marker, lifecycle-status, and force-event detail ship alongside it. This is additive: apps with no `version: { … }` entries are unaffected. See [Trail Versioning](#trail-versioning) for when to adopt it.
occurrences.reason: Preserve authored migration plans and historical decision/release evidence while keeping occurrences visible to the run ledger.

occurrences.context: `warden --fix` (exposed as `bun run warden -- --fix` in scaffolded projects) applies only `safety: 'safe'` source edits, last-to-first, and reports applied, changed-file, and skipped counts; review-required and topo diagnostics stay reported but unapplied. Newly scaffolded projects also pin `@ontrails/*` to the exact scaffolded version (no caret prerelease ranges), record a `.trails/scaffold.json` provenance breadcrumb, and reconcile existing files on `trails create` reruns instead of overwriting and failing. Existing apps inherit safe-fix on upgrade; scaffold changes only affect projects generated after upgrading.

occurrences.context: - ed888e2: Add `--token <token>` to `trails run` for permit resolution via the topo's auth adapter. New `tokenPreset()` exposes the flag; `'token'` is added to `META_FLAG_CANDIDATES`. The CLI resolves the `auth` resource through the shared permit-boundary helper, calls `adapter.authenticate({ surface: 'cli', bearerToken, requestId })`, and projects the resolved `Permit` into `ExecuteTrailOptions.permit` (added in TRL-408) so it reaches `ctx.permit`. Failures: missing adapter → `ValidationError` (exit 1); adapter returns `Err(AuthError)` or `Ok(null)` → `AuthError` (category `'auth'`, exit 9, with the adapter's code preserved on `error.context.code`). `--token` and `--permit` are mutually exclusive — supplying both yields `ValidationError`.
occurrences.reason: Preserve authored migration plans and historical decision/release evidence while keeping occurrences visible to the run ledger.

occurrences.context: - 344f2f7: Project typed-layer `input` schemas onto CLI flags. Each effective layer (topo + surface + trail composition order) with a non-undefined `input` schema gets its fields auto-derived into `--flag` options on every command it attaches to. Parsed values route to the layer at runtime via `ctx.extensions[LAYER_INPUTS_KEY][layer.name]` — `Layer.wrap` is unchanged. Collision rule: if a layer field name collides with a trail input field, another layer's projected name, or a CLI meta flag, the layer's flag is renamed to `--<layer-name>-<original-flag-name>` and a one-line warning emits to stderr (`[trails] ...`). Renames are deterministic across builds. New `LAYER_INPUTS_KEY` (exported from `@ontrails/core`) reserves the `ctx.extensions` slot.
occurrences.reason: Preserve authored migration plans and historical decision/release evidence while keeping occurrences visible to the run ledger.

occurrences.context: - 26f9ffd: Project typed-layer `input` schemas onto MCP and HTTP surfaces. Closes Phase 7. Lifts `collectAttachedTypedLayers` and `projectLayerFieldName` (collision-rename rule) into `@ontrails/core/internal/layer-projection` so all three surfaces share one source of truth. The CLI surface refactors to consume the lifted helpers (no behavior change). MCP merges layer fields into each tool's `inputSchema` and partitions inbound args at invocation time. HTTP merges layer fields into the route's request schema (query for reads, body for writes) and exposes new optional `HttpRouteDefinition.inputSchema` + `layerInputProjections` for surface adapters / OpenAPI generators. Collision rule matches TRL-473's: deterministic rename to a layer-prefixed camelCase name with the original captured in the routing table. occurrences.context: - 344f2f7: Project typed-layer `input` schemas onto CLI flags. Each effective layer (topo + surface + trail composition order) with a non-undefined `input` schema gets its fields auto-derived into `--flag` options on every command it attaches to. Parsed values route to the layer at runtime via `ctx.extensions[LAYER_INPUTS_KEY][layer.name]` — `Layer.wrap` is unchanged. Collision rule: if a layer field name collides with a trail input field, another layer's projected name, or a CLI meta flag, the layer's flag is renamed to `--<layer-name>-<original-flag-name>` and a one-line warning emits to stderr (`[trails] ...`). Renames are deterministic across builds. New `LAYER_INPUTS_KEY` (exported from `@ontrails/core`) reserves the `ctx.extensions` slot.
occurrences.reason: Preserve authored migration plans and historical decision/release evidence while keeping occurrences visible to the run ledger.

occurrences.context: - [`b077fb7`](https://github.com/outfitter-dev/trails/commit/b077fb7ba6d9724cac6f0e59bc3fec9aec28984c): Add the export-restructure Regrade class family (TRL-1210). `export-restructure:cli-aliases` inverts legacy `cliAliases`/`trailsCliAliases` exports into `surfaceOverlay({ cli })` bindings inside the module's `trailsOverlays` export — adding the `@ontrails/core` import, deleting the legacy export, and routing anything it cannot prove safe (computed keys, spreads, in-module `aliases:` references) to `needs-review` with the exact target shape named. `export-restructure:mcp-trailheads` projects call-site MCP trailhead maps into `surfaceOverlay({ mcp })` group bindings: it rewrites in place when the same module exports `trailsOverlays`, and otherwise emits a classified `needs-review` handoff naming the module-overlay target while the call-site map stays as the richer-metadata override-in-context. Warden's fix-class union grows to `'export-restructure' | 'term-rewrite'`, `no-legacy-cli-alias-export` now advertises the `export-restructure` class, and `loadWardenRegradeClasses` supersedes `loadWardenTermRewriteClasses` (still exported) as the full Warden-routed class loader. Class-mode Regrade also gains the full plan lifecycle: `trails regrade plan --type class --class-ids ...` writes a `.trails/regrade/<slug>.json` plan carrying class ids, scope, and intent, `regrade check` re-runs the dry run and gates on outstanding rewrites or review, and `regrade apply` applies and graduates the plan to `.trails/regrade/history/<slug>-<hash>.json` — the same plan → check → apply → history evidence trail vocabulary regrades already had, now available to structural transforms. The class family ships for downstream apps bridging the pre-1.0 gap, so pre-cutover alias exports and trailhead maps migrate mechanically instead of by hand.
occurrences.reason: Preserve authored migration plans and historical decision/release evidence while keeping occurrences visible to the run ledger.

The coherence rule activates on standard warden runs once fresh derivations collect app-module overlays through the shared compile channel (TRL-1209, next in this stack).

occurrences.context: - [`b077fb7`](https://github.com/outfitter-dev/trails/commit/b077fb7ba6d9724cac6f0e59bc3fec9aec28984c): Add the export-restructure Regrade class family (TRL-1210). `export-restructure:cli-aliases` inverts legacy `cliAliases`/`trailsCliAliases` exports into `surfaceOverlay({ cli })` bindings inside the module's `trailsOverlays` export — adding the `@ontrails/core` import, deleting the legacy export, and routing anything it cannot prove safe (computed keys, spreads, in-module `aliases:` references) to `needs-review` with the exact target shape named. `export-restructure:mcp-trailheads` projects call-site MCP trailhead maps into `surfaceOverlay({ mcp })` group bindings: it rewrites in place when the same module exports `trailsOverlays`, and otherwise emits a classified `needs-review` handoff naming the module-overlay target while the call-site map stays as the richer-metadata override-in-context. Warden's fix-class union grows to `'export-restructure' | 'term-rewrite'`, `no-legacy-cli-alias-export` now advertises the `export-restructure` class, and `loadWardenRegradeClasses` supersedes `loadWardenTermRewriteClasses` (still exported) as the full Warden-routed class loader. Class-mode Regrade also gains the full plan lifecycle: `trails regrade plan --type class --class-ids ...` writes a `.trails/regrade/<slug>.json` plan carrying class ids, scope, and intent, `regrade check` re-runs the dry run and gates on outstanding rewrites or review, and `regrade apply` applies and graduates the plan to `.trails/regrade/history/<slug>-<hash>.json` — the same plan → check → apply → history evidence trail vocabulary regrades already had, now available to structural transforms. The class family ships for downstream apps bridging the pre-1.0 gap, so pre-cutover alias exports and trailhead maps migrate mechanically instead of by hand.

occurrences.context: Projects can carry local Warden rules in `.trails/rules.ts` or direct `.trails/rules/*.ts` files. `runWarden()` and `trails warden` load those files by default for lint runs, then run those rules alongside the built-in registries. Drift-only runs do not import project-local rule modules. Embedders that need only built-in or explicitly provided rules can pass `projectRules: false`.

occurrences.context: The Claude plugin installs a read-only `SessionStart` hook that emits guidance only in likely Trails projects. It detects `@ontrails/*` dependencies, `package.json.trails.module`, root `trails.config.*` files, root `.trails/`, and guarded `src/app.ts`/`src/index.ts` topo sources. Outside those signals it stays silent.
occurrences.reason: Preserve projects as an ordinary plural repository or application noun selected by an established domain modifier.

occurrences.context: When a local or PATH `trails` CLI is discoverable, the hook suggests a non-mutating Warden probe with `--lock cached --no-lock-mutation`; otherwise it asks the operator to use a project-pinned `@ontrails/trails` before running Warden. The hook never syncs global skills or edits project files. Codex hook parity is not claimed here; treat this as Claude `SessionStart` behavior until verified separately.

occurrences.context: **Watch-item (release rules vs Warden):** `releaseRuleSchema` ({id, enabled, severity, description, facts, intent}) is structurally a governance rule, mirroring Warden without being Warden. ADR draft (release-provenance-as-lifecycle-projection) consciously framed release as a lifecycle *projection* (diff-provenance) distinct from Warden's state-validation axis, and exposed it as a `release.check` trail rather than a new primitive — which is correct and accepted. But two parallel rule-eval vocabularies is the ceiling. **Trigger: if a third "rules" engine appears, unify them under one governance substrate.** Two is coincidence; three is fragmentation (fights "additions strengthen primitives, not fragment them").

occurrences.context: - **Surfaces are adapters, not the core: half right, wrong word.** Surfaces aren't core; they are peers. But *adapter* is the wrong noun. An adapter translates between two systems with independent shapes; it can drift from what it adapts. A projection is a rendering of the same contract; it structurally cannot drift. Adopting "adapter" weakens the very guarantee that makes surfaces-as-peers hold.
occurrences.reason: Preserve authored migration plans and historical decision/release evidence while keeping occurrences visible to the run ledger.

occurrences.context: **The scoring frame:** An agent working *inside this repo* is at roughly 8.5/10 — queryable graph facts (Wayfinder), CI-verified docs (`docs:snippets`, `warden:agents:check`, `error-taxonomy:check`), enforced single-meaning vocabulary, a self-declared documentation authority hierarchy (tenets → ADRs → fieldguides → contributing → AGENTS.md), and in-repo agent workflow guidance (Subagent Rules, Brief Discipline). An agent in a *consumer* project is closer to 6/10. The gap is almost entirely that the repo's best legibility machinery has not been projected outward through `trails create` — a one-write-many-reads problem where the write exists and the consumer-side reads don't.

occurrences.context: **The scoring frame:** An agent working *inside this repo* is at roughly 8.5/10 — queryable graph facts (Wayfinder), CI-verified docs (`docs:snippets`, `warden:agents:check`, `error-taxonomy:check`), enforced single-meaning vocabulary, a self-declared documentation authority hierarchy (tenets → ADRs → fieldguides → contributing → AGENTS.md), and in-repo agent workflow guidance (Subagent Rules, Brief Discipline). An agent in a *consumer* project is closer to 6/10. The gap is almost entirely that the repo's best legibility machinery has not been projected outward through `trails create` — a one-write-many-reads problem where the write exists and the consumer-side reads don't.
occurrences.reason: Preserve authored migration plans and historical decision/release evidence while keeping occurrences visible to the run ledger.

occurrences.context: **2d. TRL-778 is the adoption funnel and is unprioritized.** "`trails create` detects + offers Trails plugin install (Claude Code, Codex CLI)" sits in the Fieldwork Loop backlog with no priority. This is the moment a new project's agents either get the skill or never learn it exists. Promote it.

occurrences.context: **Trails owns facts and derivation; Skillset owns lowering and distribution.** The warden guide is generated by Trails from the live rule manifest (`trails warden guide --manifest`) — that derivation must stay Trails-owned, because the facts are Trails contracts. Skillset consumes the generated output as a shared partial and projects it into every surface (SKILL.md, AGENTS.md, Codex equivalents). This is one-write-many-reads with a clean division: if Skillset ever needs to *understand* Trails facts to do its job, the seam is in the wrong place.

occurrences.context: Corollary for skill content: derive the reference layer (command catalogs, rule indexes, error taxonomies — things that exist as queryable Trails facts), author the pedagogy (narrative, examples-as-teaching, pitfalls). Over-generation is the failure mode to guard against: a skill that is 100% projection reads like a man page, not a teacher.

occurrences.context: 1. **Skill content becomes part-projection from live framework facts.** This is the big one. CLI mode consumes static source files; library mode lets Trails *synthesize Skillset source in memory* from contracts: operator topo → skill command-coverage sections (the run/add/lifecycle gap becomes derived instead of hand-authored and forever-stale); warden manifest → rule reference; error taxonomy → error-handling reference; wayfind catalog → query guidance. The skill stops being prose that drifts from the CLI and becomes a projection of it — the same move Trails made for MCP tools and CLI flags, applied to agent teaching material.

occurrences.context: 5. **In-process projection tests.** Trails' own `bun test` can assert "the skill compiled from current manifests teaches command X" without process spawning — fast, deterministic, and it makes skill coverage a *testable contract* (which is the precondition for putting skill-content claims in CI at all).

occurrences.context: CLI-mode Skillset compiles static source files a human authored. Library-mode Skillset lets a host program synthesize that source from live facts and consume the outputs as data. For Trails that converts skill content from drift-prone prose into part-projection (operator topo → command coverage, warden manifest → rule reference), enables scaffold-time tailored guidance inside `trails create`, and makes lock-hash provenance the oracle for safe guidance refresh. Details and the Trails-owns-facts/Skillset-owns-lowering seam live in the adoption note; this note is about what Skillset must *be* for any of that to work.

occurrences.context: 5. **Typed diagnostics, not thrown strings.** Lint/check/build problems as structured values with severity, source location, and a stable code — so a host can wrap them as its own check (Trails would project them into a release-smoke check or Warden-adjacent gate) instead of parsing stderr. Stable diagnostic codes matter more than message text: hosts gate on codes.

occurrences.context: This session's Warden run emitted `dead-internal-trail` for all 17 `wayfind.*` trails (`packages/wayfinder/src/queries.ts:1112-1526`). Verified: all 17 declare `visibility: 'internal'`; the rule's only escape hatches are `on:` activation or compose evidence (`packages/warden/src/rules/dead-internal-trail.ts:95-97,137-142`); nothing inspects MCP facet projection even though the trails are reachable exactly that way (`apps/trails/src/mcp-options.ts:38-46`); and no suppression, acceptance note, or TRL-915 reference exists anywhere in the repo. The project's own done-criteria say "known Warden/Wayfinder warnings are either fixed or explicitly accepted as non-blocking" — this is the bulk of them. The three remaining warnings (`signal-graph-coaching` on `demo.entity-store`) also need an accept-or-fix call; they live in the private `trails-demo` app. My recommendation: teach `dead-internal-trail` that **surface-facet include-ID projection counts as reachability** (it is declared, reviewable contract — coherent with the `surface-facet-coherence` rule already governing those maps), rather than a blanket suppression or reshaping the catalog.

occurrences.context: > **An interview is a residual-input projection.** It asks only for the parts of a trail input that remain unsatisfied after all prior sources have contributed.
occurrences.reason: Preserve authored migration plans and historical decision/release evidence while keeping occurrences visible to the run ledger.

occurrences.context: Residual input is the core primitive underneath the user-facing projection, but the lower-level machinery is **input assembly**: flags, schema defaults, env/config, prior trail outputs, example-derived suggestions, answers files, and interview answers are all just **sources contributing toward one validated input object**. The interview is whatever is left after assembly. This one sentence unifies flags+interview, defaults+interview, output→input auto-fill, agent missing-input errors, HTML form prefill, permit confirmation, and provenance.
occurrences.reason: Preserve authored migration plans and historical decision/release evidence while keeping occurrences visible to the run ledger.

occurrences.context: - **Layer inputs are part of the residual, but only when user-satisfiable.** Typed layers can declare input schemas, and surfaces already project them (the `layer-field-name-drift` rule exists because of it). Input assembly must account for layer-declared fields too, or interviews will ask for trail fields while silently missing layer fields. But not every layer field should be promptable: auth/session/tracing/runtime fields may be internal machinery. Interview asks only for layer fields that are public, user-satisfiable, and surface-projectable. One assembly, one residual, trail + eligible layers together.

occurrences.context: - **Layer-input projection (the scope gap I flagged) is NOT yet implemented.** The projection records export-level facts (name/resources/intent/version), not the input *field shape*. It becomes load-bearing in the **emitter** (TRL-969), where generated method signatures must include trail + layer fields. The fixture has no layer, so this is untested — add a layer to the fixture when the emitter lane starts.

occurrences.context: Because `derive.ts` is identical to Clark's foundation, the landed projection carries only **`trail.input`** — not layer-declared input fields. The ADR is explicit (citing ADR-0043): generated method signatures must project layer inputs alongside trail input, or they "silently drop layer fields." Today, any topo with surface-scoped layers that declare input schemas will produce generated methods missing those fields. The fixture has no layer, so it's untested and unnoticed.

occurrences.context: *Dimension 1 — layer cascade (vertical, within one named config), lowest → highest:* (1) schema defaults; (2) **global** (machine/user, `~/.config`/XDG — *missing today*); (3) **workspace-root** shared defaults (*missing — dimension 2*); (4) **project** (leaf project config, overrides root); (5) **local** (`.{name}/config.local.*`, gitignored, per-dev); (6) **env / runtime** (highest; ADR-0011 made env the override channel and deliberately rejected CLI-as-config-layer).

occurrences.context: **Boundary invariant:** if Regrade ever rewrites a *projected* output, that's a smell — the projection should have re-derived. Reach stops at the **authored** layer (trail definitions, blaze bodies); projections follow by derivation. Mature Regrade migrates *less* than a generic codemod, not more. Two tiers, both pass the test: graph tier (re-author the contract, derivation re-fans-out) + authored-source tier (AST rewrite of the words a human wrote).

occurrences.context: 1. **Surfaces are peers / opinionated authoring, standard outputs.** `summary` is the CLI's text rendering shipped as a *data field on the contract*. It reaches **every** surface — an MCP client calling `wayfind.outline` receives the CLI's prose as structured output. occurrences.context: `AGENTS.md`'s "Wayfinder First" framing presents Wayfinder as the saved-graph-facts surface — read the artifact, project facts. `outline` is the one wayfind query that parses **live source on every call**. That is defensible: the value *is* the source↔graph bridge, and Wayfinder is the graph's home, so the bridge belongs here. But the mental model ("Wayfinder = saved artifacts") now has an exception, and it should be named in the docs rather than silently true. occurrences.context: The decisive call: **the dot means "committed control," not "disposable."** Reached after weighing two coherent ways to make the dot mean exactly one thing (disposable vs committed-control); the original synthesis model failed only because it made the dot mean *both* (committed rules beside ignored cache). We picked committed-control because it gives one contained home for "what does this project do with Trails," uniform across src and flat layouts, and because the disposable meaning is recovered for free by moving disposable state out of the repo entirely (see below).
occurrences.reason: Preserve project as an ordinary count noun selected by a determiner or domain adjective.

occurrences.context: Hoist uniformly: any scope that owns a Trails project keeps `trails.config.*` + `trails.lock` at its own root (matching the per-package `package.json`/`tsconfig.json` norm), while `.trails/` holds only what has no root convention — rules and grown sections — at every scope. This dissolves any root-vs-nested asymmetry and yields a free signal: a scope with a hoisted `trails.config.*` *is* its own project root; a scope with only `.trails/rules/` is a centralized-workspace member contributing scope-local rules (it inherits the root's config). Hoisting never competes with authored `trails/` code, because `trails.*` (control) and bare `trails/` (primitive) are distinct by morphology.
occurrences.reason: Preserve project as an attributive noun before established repository and workspace concepts.

occurrences.context: Surfaces deliberately do *not* get a `src/` content dir. A surface opening is the boundary between the pure graph and the outside world — mostly projection wiring — so openings live in `bin/` (the opening and the executable entry are the same honest unit). The authored part of a surface (exposure + accommodations: aliases, input mappings, facets, per ADR-0050) attaches to the contract or `trails.config.*`, and *what's* exposed is already derivable from the topo. So there is nothing left to justify a `src/surfaces/` directory.

occurrences.context: The direction holds: a rename is a *transition*, not durable Warden state. The substrate is strong — but **not** because Warden's fix contract already carries rename intent. The missing piece is an authoring layer for transition plans — and the audit now shows exactly how small the residue is for a well-typed rename, because the framework's one-write-many-reads genuinely holds for projected surfaces.

occurrences.context: - **Slice 1 — derivation audit: DONE** (above). Headline: ID renames re-derive projections and follow typed refs; residue is untyped strings (half already detected by `validate-topo`) plus prose; coverage drops sharply for symbol and vocab renames.
occurrences.reason: Preserve authored migration plans and historical decision/release evidence while keeping occurrences visible to the run ledger.

occurrences.context: - **Slice 2 — regrade spike: DONE** — see `.agents/notes/2026-06-26-regrade-spike.md`. (The authored artifact is now **a regrade**, not a "transition plan" — vocabulary settled there.) The `kind`-carrying schema (trail-id / symbol / vocabulary), plus three things the prose side needs that a `rg`-style rewrite cannot give: morphology-aware **discovery** (`from` surfaces neighbor forms you would miss, e.g. `blazes`), per-occurrence **judgment** (a packet of intent + context + preserve-cautions + labeled examples, not a regex), and a recursive **discover → triage → judge → verify** loop driven to a completion gate (zero capture-forms AND zero unclassified neighbors). Target mapping is **derive-by-default / declare-to-tighten / override-when-wrong**. Durable retirement (`retire: true`) writes to a **typed registry**, never markdown; the home is a project binding (Trails → lexicon-rendered), never an authored noun. The primary entry is the **command** (`trails regrade <from> <to>`, **dry-run by default**, `--apply` to execute, `--plan` to scaffold a file), with Warden as the safety net for raw renames. Built **on rails**: deterministic tooling (discovery/apply/verify) + a skill (orchestration, ledger) + an agent (judgment only).

occurrences.context: - **T2-e: topographer package — KEEP as-is (census verdict, 2026-07-07).** Three genuine cross-package consumers (warden 12+ files, wayfinder 7, apps/trails 18); coherent derivation+persistence kernel (derive and topo-store share the same projection helpers) ringed by durability utilities; the boundary IS accepted **ADR-0042** (Core/Topographer Boundary Doctrine), written precisely to keep persistence out of core — dissolving would reverse a settled ADR for zero user-facing gain (concept-toll issue already solved by Tier 3 demotion). No new deps or cycles would block a dissolve (bun:sqlite is type-only; DB engine already in core), so the barrier is doctrinal, not technical — and the doctrine is right. Mild smells noted, left alone: `workspace-topos` + `activation` clusters have 1 consumer each; `deriveTopoGraph` is in-memory/single-process (by ADR-0042's own literal test reads "core") but shared helpers bind it to the persistence kernel — not worth the churn. Migration cost of dissolve: large (~55 import sites + reopening the ADR). Keep: zero.
occurrences.reason: Preserve authored migration plans and historical decision/release evidence while keeping occurrences visible to the run ledger.

The ratified projection→derive+render split supplies the grammar's two anchor verbs; core's `project*` exports resolve into derive*/render* during the reset.

occurrences.context: The marker projection is deterministic, pathful, and loud for several unsupported schema constructs, but it is not yet a safe v1 stable marker contract. The current implementation accepts common Zod validation constraints and refinements while projecting the same marker as the unconstrained schema. That means two validation contracts can be semantically different while sharing a version marker.

occurrences.context: The metadata checker, installed-skill drift checker, and Claude `SessionStart` hook are scoped and non-mutating by default. Metadata sync has a clear two-source policy: plugin manifest version owns plugin semver, `packages/core/package.json` owns the framework target, and derived marketplace/skill copies are checked or synchronized. The installed-skill checker reports copy/symlink/missing state, file drift, stale vocabulary, and `metadata.trails.version` drift without changing global files. The hook stays silent outside likely Trails projects and suggests only Warden probes with `--lock cached --no-lock-mutation`.

occurrences.context: **What's the relationship between `trails init` and `outfitter init`?** `trails init` creates a Trails project (action registry, surface adapters, contract governance). `outfitter init` sets up dev tooling (tsconfig, lefthook, oxlint). A Trails project would run both. They should compose: `trails init` could optionally call `outfitter init` for dev tooling setup, or the developer runs them independently.

occurrences.context: Add the export-restructure Regrade class family (TRL-1210). `export-restructure:cli-aliases` inverts legacy `cliAliases`/`trailsCliAliases` exports into `surfaceOverlay({ cli })` bindings inside the module's `trailsOverlays` export — adding the `@ontrails/core` import, deleting the legacy export, and routing anything it cannot prove safe (computed keys, spreads, in-module `aliases:` references) to `needs-review` with the exact target shape named. `export-restructure:mcp-trailheads` projects call-site MCP trailhead maps into `surfaceOverlay({ mcp })` group bindings: it rewrites in place when the same module exports `trailsOverlays`, and otherwise emits a classified `needs-review` handoff naming the module-overlay target while the call-site map stays as the richer-metadata override-in-context. Warden's fix-class union grows to `'export-restructure' | 'term-rewrite'`, `no-legacy-cli-alias-export` now advertises the `export-restructure` class, and `loadWardenRegradeClasses` supersedes `loadWardenTermRewriteClasses` (still exported) as the full Warden-routed class loader. Class-mode Regrade also gains the full plan lifecycle: `trails regrade plan --type class --class-ids ...` writes a `.trails/regrade/<slug>.json` plan carrying class ids, scope, and intent, `regrade check` re-runs the dry run and gates on outstanding rewrites or review, and `regrade apply` applies and graduates the plan to `.trails/regrade/history/<slug>-<hash>.json` — the same plan → check → apply → history evidence trail vocabulary regrades already had, now available to structural transforms. The class family ships for downstream apps bridging the pre-1.0 gap, so pre-cutover alias exports and trailhead maps migrate mechanically instead of by hand.

occurrences.context: Add `--token <token>` to `trails run` for permit resolution via the topo's auth adapter. New `tokenPreset()` exposes the flag; `'token'` is added to `META_FLAG_CANDIDATES`. The CLI resolves the `auth` resource through the shared permit-boundary helper, calls `adapter.authenticate({ surface: 'cli', bearerToken, requestId })`, and projects the resolved `Permit` into `ExecuteTrailOptions.permit` (added in TRL-408) so it reaches `ctx.permit`. Failures: missing adapter → `ValidationError` (exit 1); adapter returns `Err(AuthError)` or `Ok(null)` → `AuthError` (category `'auth'`, exit 9, with the adapter's code preserved on `error.context.code`). `--token` and `--permit` are mutually exclusive — supplying both yields `ValidationError`.

Layers without `input` schemas stay surface-invisible and cover runtime-only concerns such as tenant guards, rate limiting, circuit breaking, and custom audit logging.

occurrences.context: Project typed-layer `input` schemas onto CLI flags. Each effective layer (topo + surface + trail composition order) with a non-undefined `input` schema gets its fields auto-derived into `--flag` options on every command it attaches to. Parsed values route to the layer at runtime via `ctx.extensions[LAYER_INPUTS_KEY][layer.name]` — `Layer.wrap` is unchanged. Collision rule: if a layer field name collides with a trail input field, another layer's projected name, or a CLI meta flag, the layer's flag is renamed to `--<layer-name>-<original-flag-name>` and a one-line warning emits to stderr (`[trails] ...`). Renames are deterministic across builds. New `LAYER_INPUTS_KEY` (exported from `@ontrails/core`) reserves the `ctx.extensions` slot.
occurrences.reason: Preserve authored migration plans and historical decision/release evidence while keeping occurrences visible to the run ledger.

occurrences.context: Project typed-layer `input` schemas onto MCP and HTTP surfaces. Closes Phase 7. Lifts `collectAttachedTypedLayers` and `projectLayerFieldName` (collision-rename rule) into `@ontrails/core/internal/layer-projection` so all three surfaces share one source of truth. The CLI surface refactors to consume the lifted helpers (no behavior change). MCP merges layer fields into each tool's `inputSchema` and partitions inbound args at invocation time. HTTP merges layer fields into the route's request schema (query for reads, body for writes) and exposes new optional `HttpRouteDefinition.inputSchema` + `layerInputProjections` for surface adapters / OpenAPI generators. Collision rule matches TRL-473's: deterministic rename to a layer-prefixed camelCase name with the original captured in the routing table. Side fix: MCP and HTTP handlers now forward `topoLayers: graph.layers` + `surfaceLayers: layers` so topo-scope layers actually compose at runtime (previously the handlers used the deprecated `layers` alias and never read `graph.layers`).
occurrences.reason: Preserve authored migration plans and historical decision/release evidence while keeping occurrences visible to the run ledger.

This includes writing new ADRs, updating existing ones, listing or searching ADRs, and ensuring ADRs follow project conventions.\\n\\nExamples:\\n\\n- User: \"We need to document the decision to use Bun as our runtime\"\\n  Assistant: \"I'll use the maintainer agent to draft an ADR for the Bun runtime decision.\"\\n  <uses Agent tool to launch maintainer>\\n\\n- User: \"Can you update ADR-003 to reflect that we changed our mind about the storage approach?\"\\n  Assistant: \"Let me use the maintainer agent to update that ADR with the revised decision.\"\\n  <uses Agent tool to launch maintainer>\\n\\n- User: \"What ADRs do we have about our error handling strategy?\"\\n  Assistant: \"I'll use the maintainer agent to search through existing ADRs for error handling decisions.\"\\n  <uses Agent tool to launch maintainer>\\n\\n- User: \"We need to decide between Result types and exceptions — can you write up the tradeoffs?\"\\n  Assistant: \"I'll launch the maintainer agent to draft an ADR capturing the tradeoffs and proposed decision.\"\\n  <uses Agent tool to launch maintainer>"

occurrences.context: - [`b077fb7`](https://github.com/outfitter-dev/trails/commit/b077fb7ba6d9724cac6f0e59bc3fec9aec28984c): Add the export-restructure Regrade class family (TRL-1210). `export-restructure:cli-aliases` inverts legacy `cliAliases`/`trailsCliAliases` exports into `surfaceOverlay({ cli })` bindings inside the module's `trailsOverlays` export — adding the `@ontrails/core` import, deleting the legacy export, and routing anything it cannot prove safe (computed keys, spreads, in-module `aliases:` references) to `needs-review` with the exact target shape named. `export-restructure:mcp-trailheads` projects call-site MCP trailhead maps into `surfaceOverlay({ mcp })` group bindings: it rewrites in place when the same module exports `trailsOverlays`, and otherwise emits a classified `needs-review` handoff naming the module-overlay target while the call-site map stays as the richer-metadata override-in-context. Warden's fix-class union grows to `'export-restructure' | 'term-rewrite'`, `no-legacy-cli-alias-export` now advertises the `export-restructure` class, and `loadWardenRegradeClasses` supersedes `loadWardenTermRewriteClasses` (still exported) as the full Warden-routed class loader. Class-mode Regrade also gains the full plan lifecycle: `trails regrade plan --type class --class-ids ...` writes a `.trails/regrade/<slug>.json` plan carrying class ids, scope, and intent, `regrade check` re-runs the dry run and gates on outstanding rewrites or review, and `regrade apply` applies and graduates the plan to `.trails/regrade/history/<slug>-<hash>.json` — the same plan → check → apply → history evidence trail vocabulary regrades already had, now available to structural transforms. The class family ships for downstream apps bridging the pre-1.0 gap, so pre-cutover alias exports and trailhead maps migrate mechanically instead of by hand.

occurrences.context: A trail is a typed contract — input schema, output schema, error types, examples, safety properties, composition graph — that has a blaze establishing how it runs. The contract is the product. The blaze makes it runnable. CLI, MCP, HTTP, and WebSocket surfaces render it for their contexts. You author the contract once. The framework projects it onto whatever surfaces you need.

occurrences.context: The Trails lexicon has been cut over several times in the pre-1.0 window. Cutovers were applied in place across the ADR record rather than threaded through supersession, because the project is still pre-release and the old terms would only create confusion for people reading through the ADR history or for agents consuming the docs. Each cutover has its own ADR documenting the decision; this section is a running log of where the in-place rewrites happened.
occurrences.reason: Preserve project as an ordinary count noun selected by a determiner or domain adjective.

Reframed `build*` helpers as `derive*` projections, standardized `create*` for runtime materialization, and made `graph` the canonical local name for topo instances in active docs and examples. Applied in place across the active ADR record using the same pre-1.0 precedent.

occurrences.context: The framework already uses SQLite as its own internal database (see ADR: Core Database Primitive). The topo store projects the structural graph into `trails.db` for governance and introspection (see ADR: Topo Store). occurrences.context: Standard operations repeat across every domain object. CRUD for notes, CRUD for users, CRUD for gists — each is a set of trails with the same structural shape, differing only in schema and resource. The developer hand-writes five trails per entity, each with boilerplate: derive the trail ID from a convention, project input/output schemas from the store schema, set intent from the operation type, wire the resource, write a blaze that calls the store accessor.

occurrences.context: Trails has a clear convention: core primitives are bare nouns (`trail()`, `store()`, `resource()`, `signal()`), and helpers that produce primitives use verb+noun form (`createTrailContext()`, `derivePermit()`). "Derive" is the framework's own verb for mechanical projection[^1] — trail IDs from schemas, CLI flags from fields, exit codes from errors. A function that mechanically projects a trail from a schema belongs in the same category.

occurrences.context: The naming wasn't always wrong. OpenAPI generation lived in `@ontrails/schema` until the precedent was set that "schema is for primitive schema declarations, not surface-specific projections," and OpenAPI was relocated to `@ontrails/http`.[^trl-586] What's left after that move isn't primitive schema declarations — it's durable artifacts derived from the graph. The package's content is one shape; its name describes another.

occurrences.context: But the topo store is the read-only *projection of a persisted snapshot* established by [ADR-0015: Topo Store](0015-topo-store.md). It writes rows to `.trails/state/trails.db`. It tracks history across builds. It produces TopoGraph and lock-manifest exports. None of that runs to dispatch a trail; the in-memory `ReadonlyMap` is the dispatch engine.[^adr-15-execution] What ships from core today as if it were a graph primitive is, in lifecycle terms, a tooling artifact.

occurrences.context: Both misfiles point at the same missing piece: there is no single, declared rule that says when something belongs in core and when it belongs in the package next door. occurrences.context: [ADR-0051](0051-package-ownership-follows-natural-altitude.md) says a reusable capability lives in the lowest package where it is coherent. This ADR extends the doctrine from code to *concepts*: a vocabulary item belongs to the package that acts on it. `@ontrails/cli` owns what a `cli` binding means; the MCP projection owns what an `mcp` binding means; core and topography know only the overlay envelope — determinism, tolerant preservation, provenance, hash coverage. Adding accommodation kind N+1 is a schema plus a consumer; core diffs zero lines.

occurrences.context: The legacy channels are deleted, not deprecated: the `cliAliases`/`trailsCliAliases` export convention, `DeriveTopoGraphOptions.cliAliases`, the never-wired `DeriveTopoGraphOptions.trailheads` declaration option (the `surfaces` overlay's list bindings are the lock-visible carrier; Wayfinder's trailhead facts project from them), and the per-kind compile lifts. There is zero external adoption — the only consumers are in-repo, migrated in the same stack through the Regrade export-restructure class (locate export → wrap into `surfaceOverlay` → move into `trailsOverlays`), so the migration commits carry regrade plan/history evidence and future adopters crossing any pre-1.0 gap inherit the same one-command migration. occurrences.context: The packs ADR defines `pack()` as a compositional primitive. A pack carries trails, resources, signals, and config for a domain. But `pack()` says nothing about how packs travel between projects. A pack published to npm is consumed like any other dependency. A pack copied from a GitHub repo is owned source. The framework should support both modes and help developers manage the lifecycle of packs however they arrived.

occurrences.context: Use the narrowest effective authority layer. Trails should make mistakes impossible by type when it can, catch them with tests when runtime behavior is the contract, and put durable semantic checks in Warden when the invariant needs source, project, topo, or drift context. Prose or repo-local lint can orient, coach, and clean up, but they should not become parallel authority for framework facts.

occurrences.context: Project-local Warden rules live in `.trails/rules.ts` or direct `.trails/rules/*.ts` files at the project root. Warden loads those modules by default for command and API runs, so an adopting repo can enforce local migration or governance rules without shipping them from `@ontrails/warden`. The location is the ownership signal: a rule in that committed-control directory is project-local by placement, not by advisory metadata. Modules may export `rule`, `rules`, `sourceRule`, `sourceRules`, `topoRule`, or `topoRules`; rules without explicit metadata receive repo-local source-static or topo-aware execution metadata so short-lived migration rules do not need ceremony before they can run.
occurrences.reason: Preserve compound project-domain nouns, permit scopes, paths, and possessives without suppressing the standalone retired verb.

occurrences.context: Project-root discovery uses committed project markers such as `trails.config.*` or `trails.lock`, with source-shaped projects as fallback when no committed marker exists above them. occurrences.context: Beta 16 split `@ontrails/commander` out of `@ontrails/cli/commander`, moved the topo-store API into `@ontrails/topographer`, added typed layers as a real primitive with attachment scopes, renamed surface-map artifacts to `TopoGraph` / `.trails/topo.lock`, added the `unmockable` resource marker, and absorbed pagination and date shortcuts into CLI surface derivation. Beta 17 added the example-driven surface-parity helper and the HTTP surface harness, and projected `inputSchema` v1 minimums for shipped surface entrypoints. Beta 18 added `@ontrails/http/fetch` and `@ontrails/http/bun` as a Web Fetch kernel plus a Bun-native HTTP binding. Beta 19 renamed the `cross` composition family to `compose`, promoted the topo artifact commands to top-level `trails compile` / `trails validate`, made trail versioning resolve at runtime across all surfaces, introduced the `@ontrails/adapter-kit` authoring toolchain, and added `warden --fix` for safe source fixes. Together those changes mean a downstream app upgrading from beta.15 should rethink CLI imports, MCP exposure, public output schemas, resource mocks, error taxonomy, observability, Topographer adoption, and trail-composition vocabulary, not just bump versions.
occurrences.reason: Preserve authored migration plans and historical decision/release evidence while keeping occurrences visible to the run ledger.

occurrences.context: Beta 19 renames the first-class trail composition family from `cross` to `compose` across core contracts, testing helpers, topo projections, Warden rules, and CLI scaffolds. `composes`, `ctx.compose`, `composeInput`, and `Compose*` type names are now the public authoring vocabulary. Topo persistence migrates legacy composition rows and graph keys forward on the next `trails compile`, so committed artifacts re-key themselves.
occurrences.reason: Preserve authored migration plans and historical decision/release evidence while keeping occurrences visible to the run ledger.

occurrences.context: Rename every `crosses:` declaration to `composes:`, every `ctx.cross(...)` call to `ctx.compose(...)`, and any imported `Cross*` / `crossInput` symbols to their `Compose*` / `composeInput` equivalents. After renaming, run `bun run compile` to re-key the committed `.trails/topo.lock`, then `bun run validate` to confirm no drift remains. (Scaffolded projects expose the framework commands as package scripts — `bun run compile`, `bun run validate`, `bun run diff` — so prefer those over `bun trails …`, which only works where a `trails` script exists; otherwise use `bunx trails …`.)
occurrences.reason: Preserve authored migration plans and historical decision/release evidence while keeping occurrences visible to the run ledger.

occurrences.context: Trail versioning is no longer authoring-only. Beta 19 resolves trail versions during execution — live revisions, forks, marker references, and unsupported-version errors — and projects live version metadata on CLI, HTTP, and MCP surfaces with explicit surface version selection threaded through shared execution. A version lifecycle CLI — `trails revise`, `trails deprecate` (with `--archive` to archive instead of deprecate), and `trails doctor` for diagnostics — and a top-level `trails diff` that surfaces version, marker, lifecycle-status, and force-event detail ship alongside it. This is additive: apps with no `version: { … }` entries are unaffected. See [Trail Versioning](#trail-versioning) for when to adopt it.
occurrences.reason: Preserve authored migration plans and historical decision/release evidence while keeping occurrences visible to the run ledger.

occurrences.context: `warden --fix` (exposed as `bun run warden -- --fix` in scaffolded projects) applies only `safety: 'safe'` source edits, last-to-first, and reports applied, changed-file, and skipped counts; review-required and topo diagnostics stay reported but unapplied. Newly scaffolded projects also pin `@ontrails/*` to the exact scaffolded version (no caret prerelease ranges), record a `.trails/scaffold.json` provenance breadcrumb, and reconcile existing files on `trails create` reruns instead of overwriting and failing. Existing apps inherit safe-fix on upgrade; scaffold changes only affect projects generated after upgrading.

occurrences.context: This is DRY applied not just to code, but to information. Frameworks have always been good at eliminating duplicate code. Trails extends that principle to duplicate authorship — across the entire surface area of a project, from the implementation to the CLI to the MCP tools to the tests to the agent documentation.
